tips: Connects the two halves of the sentence
Often gives a sense of dwelling in a particular way
viharati = to dwell or live in a certain state
From loka: the world or sphere of existence
Shows where the grasping would occur
Full stem: upa (final vowel elided in sandhi before ādi)
Upādiyati = takes up or clings to
Common framing verb for stating a fact or condition
From kāya: accumulation or group; here the physical body
Presents this as one possible formulation
Shifts attention or adds a nuance
Same stem as earlier in the sutta but here as the subject being established
Lit. stood near; the state of mindfulness being ready
The verb that completes the clause
Only to the extent that / just enough
Limits the purpose of the mindfulness
For the measure of / only for
For the measure of / only for

phases.pali: ekaṁ samayaṁ bhagavā kurūsu viharati kammāsadhammaṁ nāma kurūnaṁ nigamo.

tips: Name of a specific town in the Kuru country
The proper name is kept intact as one segment
The ending marks the object of the phrase
Here it completes the name of the town being described
Links proper name to common noun
By the name of or namely
Common word used to introduce a proper name
The Kurus — name of a people and their land
Same stem as earlier in this phase, now showing belonging
Pāli marks belonging directly on the noun
"A town" — what we are talking about
Often used in words for settling or a market town
With ni- it forms the idea of a place people go to — a market town
The ending marks it as the subject of the clause
Makes the whole phrase 'a market town of the Kurus'
The ending marks a point in time
Intensifies the idea of coming together
From the root meaning to go
From the root meaning to share or apportion
The Kurus — name of a people and their land
Same stem appears later in this phase with different endings
The ending means 'among' or 'in the land of'
Apart or in a certain way
Often gives the sense of dwelling or being in a state
The root meaning to carry or bear

phases.pali: “bhikkhavo”ti.

tips: the root 'bhikkh' related to begging or living on alms
specifically religious mendicancy, not ordinary poverty
used when calling out to a group of monks respectfully

phases.pali: Bhagavā etadavoca:

tips: The stem 'bhagavant' — one who possesses fortune or shares the Dhamma
Common epithet for the Buddha across many suttas
Nominative singular ending — marks the subject of the sentence
This form appears in many narrative phases as the speaker
This very thing — neuter pronoun referring to what follows
Common in formulas that introduce quoted speech
Augment 'a-' — marks this as a past-tense form

phases.pali: Katame cattāro?

tips: The -e ending marks nominative plural masculine
Makes the pronoun agree with the noun it questions
The cardinal number 4 in nominative form
The -o ending marks nominative plural masculine
Agrees with the implied subject of the question

phases.pali: Idha, bhikkhave, bhikkhu kāye kāyānupassī viharati ātāpī sampajāno satimā, vineyya loke abhijjhādomanassaṁ;

tips: "Body in body" = seeing body AS body
The -e ending marks where the observing happens
The -e ending means 'in the body' — Pāli puts the location on the noun itself
Identity shift: you become an observer
Long ā from sandhi with following part
From anu + √dis: to see / to regard
anu = along / following; the whole means observing or watching closely
The -ī ending makes it 'one who observes' — turns the action into a description of the person
vi-: apart / in a certain way
Often intensifies or gives direction to the root
vi + har = to stay / to dwell in a place
Thematic vowel that links root to ending
The -ti ending marks third-person singular present — 'he dwells'
Stem shared with bhikkhu — same word as the subject but now calling out to the group
Used when addressing multiple people respectfully
The -e ending calls out to a group — used when addressing monks directly
Lit. one who begs for alms — the same stem as the vocative just before but now the subject
Refers to the burning away of mental defilements through effort
The -ī ending means 'one who has' this quality — 'ardent one'
From √sar: to remember / to be mindful
The -mā ending (from -mant) means 'possessing' — one who has mindfulness
vi + nī = to remove / to put aside
loka: world / the world of people
The -e ending marks where the covetousness is located
The -e ending means 'in the world' — Pāli marks the location directly on the noun
